content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: noun + **be** in the correct form + past participle (+ **by**/**with** + noun)

rows: present simple
*Scientists launch satellites into orbit every year.*

am/is/are + past participle
*Satellites **are launched** into orbit every year.*
present continuous
*Engineers are upgrading the old railway bridge.*

am/is/are + -ing + past participle
*The old railway bridge **is being upgraded**.*
present perfect simple
*Has anyone translated this ancient manuscript?*

has/have + been + past participle
***Has** this ancient manuscript **been translated**?*
past simple
*They performed the symphony in a grand concert hall.*

was/were + past participle
*The symphony **was performed** in a grand concert hall.*
past continuous
*We waited outside while they were setting up the stage.*

was/were + -ing + past participle
*We waited outside while the stage **was being set up**.*
past perfect simple
*An athlete had broken the world record by the time the Games ended.*

had + been + past participle
*The world record **had been broken** by the time the Games ended.*
will future
*The team will launch the new spacecraft in six months.*

will + be + past participle
*The new spacecraft **will be launched** in six months.*
be going to future
*Local campaigners are going to challenge the new motorway development.*

is/are going to + be + past participle
*The new motorway development **is going to be challenged** by local campaigners.*
future perfect simple
*Researchers will have completed all the archaeological excavations by the end of summer.*

will + have + been + past participle
*All the archaeological excavations **will have been completed** by the end of summer.*
modal
*You should clean the telescope lens carefully before using it.*

modal + be + past participle
*The telescope lens **should be cleaned** carefully before use.*
modal + perfect infinitive
*They should have published the research findings by now.*

modal + have + been + past participle
*The research findings **should have been published** by now.*
-ing (gerund)
*I don't like people interrupting me during rehearsals.*

being + past participle
*I don't like **being interrupted** during rehearsals.*

content.subtitle: Use
items: When we don't know who does/did something: *My bicycle **has been stolen**!*
When it's obvious who does/did something: *A teenager **was arrested** near the park yesterday for vandalism.*
When it's not important who does/did something: *The old theatre **is being knocked down**.*
When we want to emphasise new information or use a formal style: *The telephone **was brought** to the world by Alexander Graham Bell.*

items.note: We do not normally use verbs in the passive in the present perfect continuous, past perfect continuous, future continuous or future perfect continuous tenses. Instead, we use a different phrase.

items.correct: The bridge has been **under construction** for four years.

items.incorrect: The bridge has been being built for four years.

items.correct: The athlete has been **in training** for the marathon for three years.

items.incorrect: The athlete has been being trained for the marathon for three years.

items.note: We only normally use 'by' to say who did something when it is important information.

items.correct: The telephone was invented **by** Alexander Graham Bell.
The finest violins are made **by** Italian craftsmen.

items.incorrect: Conductor! This piece has been played incorrectly by someone.

items.note: We usually use 'with' when we talk about the thing used to do something.

items.correct: The wound should then be cleaned **with** a cloth.

items.incorrect: The wound should be cleaned by a cloth.

items.note: Some verbs are not normally used in the passive. They include intransitive verbs (without objects), such as *appear* and *die*, and some common transitive verbs, such as *have*, *let*, *lack*, etc.

content.description: To express other people's opinions in a formal style, we can use two special forms of the passive. They can be used with a number of verbs, including: *say, believe, think, claim, estimate,* etc. Some other verbs (*argue, suggest, calculate,* etc) are usually used with only the second structure.

content.subtitle: Form
items: noun + *is/are said to* + bare infinitive/perfect infinitive

rows: People **think** he **is** an outstanding astronomer.

He **is thought to be** an outstanding astronomer.
It **is thought that** he **is** an outstanding astronomer.
People **believe** he **was** a pioneering scientist.

He **is believed to have been** a pioneering scientist.
It **is believed that** he **was** a pioneering scientist.
People **claim** he **has had** an influence on many other researchers.

He **is claimed to have had** an influence on many other researchers.
It **is claimed that** he **has had** an influence on many other researchers.
People **say** he **has been conducting** groundbreaking research in the field for over thirty years.

He **is said to have been conducting** groundbreaking research in the field for over thirty years.
It **is said that** he **has been conducting** groundbreaking research in the field for over thirty years.
People **estimated** that his laboratory **was** worth over $10 million.

His laboratory **was estimated to be** / **to have been** worth over $10 million.
It **was estimated** that his laboratory **was** worth over $10 million.
People **have suggested** that he **is** the most important physicist of his generation.
It **has been suggested** that he **is** the most important physicist of his generation.

content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: noun + **have**/**get** in the correct form + noun + past participle (+ **by**/**with** + noun)

rows.label: To show that someone arranges for someone else to do something for them
items: I **have** my car **serviced** at the garage once a year.
We **are having** a new security system **installed** tomorrow.
We **had** a professional designer **hired** for the project.
We **are going to have** the stage set **built** by a specialist company.

rows.label: To refer to an unpleasant situation which hasn't been arranged
items: We **had** our car **broken into** while we were at the concert.
The Robinsons **have had** their new laptop **stolen**.

items.note: Using the verb *get* is usually more informal than using *have*.

items.correct: Can you go and **get** this document **photocopied** for me?

items.note: We can also use *get somebody to do* and *have somebody do* when we want to refer to the person we arrange to do something for us.

items.correct: Why don't you **get the technician to repair** your computer?
Why don't you **have the technician repair** your computer?

content.description:
Some verbs can be followed by both a direct and an indirect object (usually a person). These verbs include:
*bring, buy, get, give, lend, make, offer, owe, pass, promise, send, show, take, teach, tell, write,* etc.

rows:
We can put the indirect object either immediately after the verb, or at the end of the sentence with a preposition (*for*/*to*, etc.).
A colleague lent **my brother** this history book.
A colleague lent this history book **to my brother**.

The subject of the sentence can be either the indirect object or the direct object of the active sentence.
**My brother** was lent this history book by a colleague.
**This history book** was lent to my brother by a colleague.
